intro:
The lampstand or menorah (Hebrew mĕnôrāh) was the seven-branched golden lamp that stood in the Holy Place of the tabernacle and temple. Hammered from a single talent of pure gold and shaped like a flowering almond tree — with cups, calyxes, and blossoms on its branches — it held seven lamps that were tended to give light continually before the LORD (Exodus 25:31–37). It was the sole source of light in that windowless sanctuary, and its flame was never to go out.

Its meaning grows from its setting and its form. Standing in God's house, burning perpetually, the menorah signified the abiding presence and life of God among his people: the priests were to keep the lamps burning "from evening to morning before the LORD regularly" (Exodus 27:20–21; Leviticus 24:2–4). Its almond-tree shape — the first tree to blossom in spring — joined the imagery of the tree of life with the gift of light, picturing a flourishing, God-given radiance at the heart of the sanctuary. Light in Scripture is the sphere of God himself: "the LORD is my light" (Psalm 27:1).

The prophet Zechariah is shown a golden lampstand fed continually with oil from two olive trees, and the vision is interpreted: "Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the LORD of hosts" (Zechariah 4:2–3; Zechariah 4:6). The lamp shines not by its own resources but by the unfailing supply of God's Spirit — the oil that keeps the light alive.

This shadow finds its fulfillment in the Church. The risen Christ appears "in the midst of the lampstands," and he declares plainly, "the seven lampstands are the seven churches" (Revelation 1:12–13; Revelation 1:20). The single menorah of the old sanctuary opens out into many golden lampstands — congregations of God's people — among whom Christ himself walks. Jesus is the light (John 8:12); the Church is the lampstand that bears and displays his light in the world, and so he charges his people, "You are the light of the world… let your light shine before others" (Matthew 5:14–16). As in Zechariah's vision, the Church shines only by the oil of the Spirit, never by its own power — and a lampstand that ceases to shine the light of Christ may be "removed from its place" (Revelation 2:5).

The lampstand matters because it shows the calling of God's people: not to be the light, but to hold it high. Where once a single golden lamp burned in a curtained room, now the Church — filled with the Spirit and indwelt by the risen Christ — is set in the open world to give the continual, undimmed light of God to all who sit in darkness.
